en.extract:
The grey-headed fish eagle (Icthyophaga ichthyaetus) is a fish-eating bird of prey from Southeast Asia. It is a large stocky raptor with adults having dark brown upper body, grey head and lighter underbelly and white legs. Juveniles are paler with darker streaking. It is often confused with the lesser fish eagle (Icthyophaga humilis) and the Pallas's fish eagle. The lesser fish eagle is similar in plumage but smaller and the Pallas's fish eagle shares the same habitat and feeding behaviour but is larger with longer wings and darker underparts. Is often called tank eagle in Sri Lanka due to its fondness for irrigation tanks.


Taxonomy
The grey-headed fish eagle was first described by Horsfield in 1841 as Falco ichthyaetus. It is included in the order Accipitriformes and the family Accipitridae, which includes most birds of prey except for the ospreys and falcons. Lerner & Mindell placed the grey-headed fish eagle in the subfamily Haliaeetinae, which includes the genera Haliaeetus (sea eagles) This paraphyletic group forms a close sister relationship with the subfamily Milvinae, based on the shared trait of basal fusion of the second and third phalanges found only in these two groups.


Description

The grey-headed fish eagle is a smallish to medium-sized but quite bulky fish eagle, with a small bill, a small head on long neck, rounded tail and shortish legs with unfeathered tarsi and long talons. Wings are not very long and wingtips reach less than halfway down tail. Males and females are sexually dimorphic. The grey-headed fish eagle has a body length of 61–75 cm (24–30 in). Females are heavier than males at 2.3–2.7 kg (5.1–6.0 lb) compared to 1.6 kg (3.5 lb). The tail measures between 23–28 cm (9.1–11.0 in) and the tarsus 8.5–10 cm (3.3–3.9 in). The wingspan measures between 155 and 170 cm (61 and 67 in).
Adults are grey-brown with a pale grey head and pale iris, belly and tail are white with the having a broad black subterminal band.  Breast and neck are brown, with the wings on top dark brown with blacker primaries and below brown. Juveniles the head and neck are brown, greyer on the ides of throat, with buff supercilia and whitish streaks. The rest of the upperparts are darker brown, edged with grey and secondaries and tertials faintly barred. Tail black and white marbled with broader dark subterminal band and white tip.  Belly and thighs white, while breast and flanks brown streaked with white. Iris is darker than adult.  As juveniles mature subterminal band becomes more prominent, head becomes greyer and loses streaking becoming uniformly brown.


Distribution and habitat
The grey-headed fish eagle has a wide distribution (38˚ N to 6˚ S) that encompasses India and South-East Asia to Malaysia, Western Indonesia and Philippines. It is generally uncommon but can be rare or local. In India, it occurs in Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and Assam. It is uncommon in North and East Sri Lanka, rare and local in Nepal and uncommon and local in Bangladesh. It is rare and local in South Thailand and rare in Laos; scarce in Vietnam, Cambodia and Malaysia to Sumatra; very rare in Java and Sulawesi except for a small local population and scarce in Borneo and the Philippines.
The grey-headed fish eagle inhabits lowland forest up to an elevation of 1,500 m (4,900 ft). It builds nests close to bodies of water such as slow-moving rivers and streams, lakes, lagoons, reservoirs, marshes, swamps and coastal lagoons and estuaries. It also frequents irrigation tanks in Sri Lanka.


Ecology and behaviour
The grey-headed fish eagle is sedentary that can be solitary or occur in pairs. It is non-migratory. Juveniles disperse from the breeding areas, presumably in search of mates or another food source. The grey headed fish eagle spends much of its time perching upright on bare branches over water bodies, occasionally flying down to catch fish. Flight is heavy looking with sharp and full wing-beats on flattish wings.


Breeding
The breeding season of the grey-headed fish eagle is usually between November and May across most of its mainland range, but changes from December to March in Sri Lanka, November to January in India. Nests have been found in January–March in Burma, April in Sumatra and August in Borneo, it is unclear whether these nest were old or being used for breeding. Breeding in the protected Prek Toal area of the Tonlé Sap follow the flood regimes that begin in September, with eggs near hatching or hatching at peak flood waters in October–November.
The grey-headed fish eagle builds a huge stick nest, up to 1.5 m (4 ft 11 in) across and, with repeated use, up to 2 m (6 ft 7 in) deep. The nest is lined with green leaves and were situated in 8–30 m (26–98 ft) tall trees of on or near the top of the tree with an open crown structure, which can be in a forest or a standalone tree. Nest sites were always near or by a water source with the avoidance of human habitations and is consistent with other fish eagles due to ease of access and food abundance.
The clutch size can be between two and four eggs but usually two unmarked white eggs are laid per couple. Little is known about the level of parental care employed by the grey headed fish eagle, the evidence points towards monogamy and shared parental care duties. Both incubation, foraging and fledgling feeding are carried out by the male and female, with incubation lasting 45–50 days and the fledgling period 70 days.


Foraging and diet
As the common name suggests the grey-headed fish eagle is a specialist piscivore, which preys upon live fish and scavenges dead fish and occasionally reptiles and terrestrial birds and small mammals. The diet of the grey-headed fish eagle in Prek Toal protected area of the Tonlé Sap contains the endangered Tonlé Sap water snake. Whether this is the primary prey item of their diet or a seasonal occurrence in this are remains unclear.
The most common method of foraging used is to catch fish from a hunting perch close to a water source with a short flight to snatch prey on the water surface or just below.  Also quarters over stretches of river or lakes and fish too heavy to lift may be dragged to bank to devour. It is also dynamic in prey pursuit and can catch fish in rough water such as rapids. Both species in the genus Icthyophaga have strongly recurved talons, a specialisation for catching fish, which is lacking in the genus Haliaeetus.

In the Sikundur Monitoring Post in Gunung Leuser National Park, the grey-headed fish eagle is a potential predator of the Thomas's langur, lar gibbon, southern pig-tailed macaque, crab-eating macaque, Sumatran orangutan and siamang.


Voice
The calls of the grey-headed fish eagle include a gurgling awh-awhr and chee-warr repeated 5–6 times, an owlish ooo-wok, ooo-wok, ooo-wok, a nasally honking uh-wuk and a loud high pitched scream. These begin as subdued low short notes each succeeding one more strongly upturned and more strident then previous then dying away again and are uttered from a perch or on the wing. Fledglings give a longer nasal uuuw-whaar that starts low and subdued then becomes, louder and higher and strident.  During the breeding season becomes quite vocal, with calls being loud and far carrying, often calling also at night.


Threats
Although not currently considered to be threatened with extinction, the population of grey-headed fish eagles is declining, the result of numerous and varied threats. The loss of suitable wetland habitat, deforestation, over-fishing, siltation, persecution, human disturbance and pollution resulting in a loss of nesting sites and reduced food supply. Tingay et al. noted that these statements are based mostly on anecdotal evidence, although their studies found a definitive negative link between human habitation and grey-headed fish eagle nest occupancy rates in Cambodia. Another critical threat to the Cambodian populations of the grey-headed fish eagle on the Tonlé Sap is the damming of the Mekong River for hydropower, which will possibly have adverse effects on critical flood regimes of the Tonlé Sap.


Conservation status
The grey-headed fish eagle is currently listed as Near-Threatened on the IUCN Red List. The population is estimated to be between 10,000–100,000 mature individuals on the basis that it may not exceed a five figure total. This estimate was completed in 2001 with poor data quality, combined with a marked decrease in populations is would be reasonable to assume that the number is closer to 10,000 and bordering on being classified Vulnerable. The population is spread out over 5 million km² and is now thought be only common locally, with moderate rapid population decline throughout its range.
Although there are no active conservation measures currently in place for the grey-headed fish eagle, there is an annual monitoring programme for the breeding population in the Prek Toal protected area at the Tonlé Sap Lake in Cambodia, which has been conducted each year since 2006. The programme provides baseline information on the ecology of the species and the status and distribution of the breeding population. A number of other conservation actions have also been proposed by the IUCN, these include surveys to reveal important areas and regularly monitor at various sites throughout its range, protect forest in areas known to be important to the species and conduct awareness campaigns involving local residents to engender pride in the species and encourage better care of wetland areas.


References

The '''grey-headed fish eagle''' ('''''Icthyophaga ichthyaetus''''') is a fish-eating bird of prey from Southeast Asia.<ref name=Robson>{{cite book |last1=Robson |first1=C. |title=A Field Guide to the Birds of South-East Asia |publisher=New Holland Publishers |location=UK |year=2000}}</ref> It is a large stocky raptor with adults having dark brown upper body, grey head and lighter underbelly and white legs.<ref name=Rasmussen>{{cite book |last1=Rasmussen |first1=P. C. |author2=Anderton, J. C. |title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide |publisher=Smithsonian Institution and Lynx Edicions |location=Washington D.C. and Barcelona |year=2005 |volume=1–2}}</ref> Juveniles are paler with darker streaking. It is often confused with the [[lesser fish eagle]] (''Icthyophaga humilis'') and the [[Pallas's fish eagle]]. The lesser fish eagle is similar in [[plumage]] but smaller and the Pallas's fish eagle shares the same habitat and feeding behaviour but is larger with longer wings and darker underparts. Is often called tank eagle in Sri Lanka due to its fondness for irrigation tanks.<ref name=Ferguson>{{cite book |last1=Ferguson-Lees |first1=J. |author2=Christie, D. A. |title=Raptors of the World |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London, UK |year=2001}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The grey-headed fish eagle was first described by Horsfield in 1841 as ''Falco ichthyaetus''.<ref name=Thiollay1994>Thiollay, J. M. (1994). "Family Accipitridae (Hawks and Eagles)", pp. 52–205 in J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott & J. Sargatal (Eds.) ''Handbook of the Birds of the World''. Vol. 2. New World Vultures to Guineafowl. Vol. 2 . Barcelona: Lynx Ediciones.</ref> It is included in the order [[Accipitriformes]] and the family [[Accipitridae]], which includes most birds of prey except for the [[osprey]]s and [[falcons]].<ref name=Ferguson /> Lerner & Mindell placed the grey-headed fish eagle in the subfamily [[Haliaeetinae]], which includes the genera ''[[Haliaeetus]]'' (sea eagles)<ref name=Lerner>{{cite journal| doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2005.04.010 |last1=Lerner |first1=H. R. L. |last2=Mindell| first2=D. P. |date=2005 |title=Phylogeny of eagles, Old World vultures, and other Accipitridae based on nuclear and mitochondrial DNA |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=37 |issue=2 |pages=327–346 |pmid=15925523 |bibcode=2005MolPE..37..327L |url=http://www-personal.umich.edu/~hlerner/LM2005.pdf |access-date=2015-09-04 |archive-date=2011-06-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110606125031/http://www-personal.umich.edu/~hlerner/LM2005.pdf}}</ref> This [[Paraphyly|paraphyletic]] group forms a close sister relationship with the subfamily [[Milvinae]], based on the shared trait of basal fusion of the second and third phalanges found only in these two groups.<ref name=Lerner2007>{{cite thesis |author=Lerner, H. R. L. |year=2007 |title=Molecular phylogenetics of diurnal birds of prey in the avian Accipitridae family |type=Ph.D. Thesis |publisher=University of Michigan |place=Ann Arbor |url=http://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/handle/2027.42/57684}}</ref>

== Description ==
[[File:Grey-headed Fish Eagle in Kaziranga National Park March 2025 by Tisha Mukherjee.mpg|thumb|A grey-headed fish eagle in [[Kaziranga National Park]]]]
The grey-headed fish eagle is a smallish to medium-sized but quite bulky fish eagle, with a small bill, a small head on long neck, rounded tail and shortish legs with unfeathered tarsi and long talons. Wings are not very long and wingtips reach less than halfway down tail. Males and females are sexually dimorphic. The grey-headed fish eagle has a body length of {{cvt|61–75|cm}}.<ref name=Thiollay1994 /> Females are heavier than males at {{cvt|2.3–2.7|kg}} compared to {{cvt|1.6|kg}}.<ref name=Ferguson /> The tail measures between {{cvt|23–28|cm}} and the tarsus {{cvt|8.5–10|cm}}.<ref name=Ferguson /> The wingspan measures between {{cvt|155|and|170|cm}}.<ref name=Thiollay1994 />

Adults are grey-brown with a pale grey head and pale iris, belly and tail are white with the having a broad black subterminal band.<ref name=Rasmussen />  Breast and neck are brown, with the wings on top dark brown with blacker primaries and below brown.<ref name=Ferguson /> Juveniles the head and neck are brown, greyer on the ides of throat, with buff supercilia and whitish streaks.<ref name=Ferguson /> The rest of the upperparts are darker brown, edged with grey and secondaries and tertials faintly barred.<ref name=Rasmussen /><ref name=Ferguson /> Tail black and white marbled with broader dark subterminal band and white tip.<ref name=Rasmussen />  Belly and thighs white, while breast and flanks brown streaked with white.<ref name=Ferguson /> Iris is darker than adult.<ref name=Rasmussen />  As juveniles mature subterminal band becomes more prominent, head becomes greyer and loses streaking becoming uniformly brown.<ref name=Ferguson/>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The grey-headed fish eagle has a wide distribution (38˚ N to 6˚ S) that encompasses India and South-East Asia to Malaysia, Western Indonesia and Philippines.<ref name=Ferguson /> It is generally uncommon but can be rare or local. In India, it occurs in Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and Assam.<ref name=Ferguson /> It is uncommon in North and East Sri Lanka,<ref name=Ferguson /> rare and local in Nepal and uncommon and local in Bangladesh.<ref name=Rasmussen /> It is rare and local in South Thailand and rare in Laos;<ref name=Ferguson /> scarce in Vietnam, Cambodia and Malaysia to Sumatra;<ref name=Thiollay1998>{{Cite journal |doi=10.1046/j.1365-2699.1998.251164.x| title = Distribution patterns and insular biogeography of South Asian raptor communities |journal=Journal of Biogeography |volume=25 |pages=52–72 |year=1998 |last1=Thiollay |first1=J. M. |issue=1 |bibcode=1998JBiog..25...52T}}</ref><ref name=Tingay2006>{{Cite journal | doi = 10.3356/0892-1016(2006)40[277:SADOTG]2.0.CO;2| title=Status and Distribution of the Grey-Headed Fish-Eagle (''Ichthyophaga ichthyaetus'') in the Prek Toal Core Area of Tonle Sap Lake, Cambodia |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=40 |issue=4 |page=277 |year=2006| last1 = Tingay | first1 = R. E. | last2 = Nicoll |first2 = M. A. C. |last3=Visal |first3=S. |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Tingay2010>{{Cite journal |doi=10.3356/jrr-09-04.1 |title=Nesting Ecology of the Grey-Headed Fish-Eagle at Prek Toal, Tonle Sap Lake, Cambodia |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=44 |issue=3 |page=165 |year=2010 |last1=Tingay |first1=R. E. |last2=Nicoll |first2=M. A. C. | last3 = Whitfield | first3 = D. P. |last4=Visal |first4=S. |last5=McLeod |first5=D. R. A. |s2cid=84491625 |doi-access=free}}</ref> very rare in Java and Sulawesi except for a small local population and scarce in Borneo and the Philippines.<ref name=Ferguson /><ref name=Thiollay2002>{{Cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0006-3207(02)00051-4 |title=The raptor community of Central Sulawesi: Habitat selection and conservation status |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=107 |pages=111–122 |year=2002 |last1=Thiollay |first1=J. M. |last2=Rahman |first2=Z. |issue=1 |bibcode=2002BCons.107..111T}}</ref>

The grey-headed fish eagle inhabits lowland forest up to an elevation of {{cvt|1500|m}}.<ref name=Ferguson/> It builds nests close to bodies of water such as slow-moving rivers and streams, lakes, lagoons, reservoirs, marshes, swamps and coastal lagoons and estuaries.<ref name=Ferguson /><ref name=Tingay2006 /> It also frequents irrigation tanks in Sri Lanka.<ref name=Rasmussen />

== Ecology and behaviour ==
The grey-headed fish eagle is sedentary that can be solitary or occur in pairs. It is non-migratory. Juveniles disperse from the breeding areas, presumably in search of mates or another food source. The grey headed fish eagle spends much of its time perching upright on bare branches over water bodies, occasionally flying down to catch fish.<ref name=Ferguson /> Flight is heavy looking with sharp and full wing-beats on flattish wings.<ref name=Ferguson />

=== Breeding ===
The breeding season of the grey-headed fish eagle is usually between November and May across most of its mainland range, but changes from December to March in Sri Lanka, November to January in India.<ref name=Ferguson /> Nests have been found in January–March in Burma, April in Sumatra and August in Borneo, it is unclear whether these nest were old or being used for breeding.<ref name=Thiollay1994/> Breeding in the protected Prek Toal area of the [[Tonlé Sap]] follow the flood regimes that begin in September, with eggs near hatching or hatching at peak flood waters in October–November.<ref name=Tingay2006 />

The grey-headed fish eagle builds a huge stick nest, up to {{cvt|1.5|m}} across and, with repeated use, up to {{cvt|2|m}} deep.<ref name=Ferguson /> The nest is lined with green leaves and were situated in {{cvt|8–30|m}} tall trees of on or near the top of the tree with an open crown structure, which can be in a forest or a standalone tree.<ref name=Ferguson /><ref name=Tingay2010 /> Nest sites were always near or by a water source with the avoidance of human habitations and is consistent with other fish eagles due to ease of access and food abundance.<ref name=Tingay2010 />

The [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] size can be between two and four eggs but usually two unmarked white eggs are laid per couple.<ref name=Ferguson /> Little is known about the level of parental care employed by the grey headed fish eagle, the evidence points towards monogamy and shared parental care duties. Both incubation, foraging and fledgling feeding are carried out by the male and female, with incubation lasting 45–50 days and the fledgling period 70 days.<ref name=Ferguson /><ref name=Tingay2010 />

=== Foraging and diet ===
As the common name suggests the grey-headed fish eagle is a specialist [[piscivore]], which preys upon live fish and scavenges dead fish<ref name=Tingay2006 /> and occasionally reptiles and terrestrial birds and small mammals.<ref name=Ferguson /> The diet of the grey-headed fish eagle in Prek Toal protected area of the [[Tonlé Sap]] contains the endangered Tonlé Sap water snake.<ref name=Tingay2006 /> Whether this is the primary prey item of their diet or a seasonal occurrence in this are remains unclear.
The most common method of foraging used is to catch fish from a hunting perch close to a water source with a short flight to snatch prey on the water surface or just below.<ref name=Thiollay1994 />  Also quarters over stretches of river or lakes and fish too heavy to lift may be dragged to bank to devour.<ref name=Ferguson /> It is also dynamic in prey pursuit and can catch fish in rough water such as rapids.<ref name=Rasmussen /> Both species in the genus ''Icthyophaga'' have strongly recurved talons, a specialisation for catching fish, which is lacking in the genus ''Haliaeetus''.<ref name=Poole>{{cite book |author=Poole, A. F. |year=1989 |title=Ospreys: A natural and unnatural history |place=Cambridge, U.K. |publisher=Cambridge University Press}}</ref>

[[File:Grey-headed fish eagle.jpg|thumb|Grey-headed fish eagle with a freshly caught tilapia fish]]
In the Sikundur Monitoring Post in [[Gunung Leuser National Park]], the grey-headed fish eagle is a potential predator of the [[Thomas's langur]], [[lar gibbon]], [[southern pig-tailed macaque]], [[crab-eating macaque]], [[Sumatran orangutan]] and [[siamang]].<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Harrison, N. J. |author2=Hill, R. A. |author3=Alexander, C. |author4=Marsh, C. D. |author5=Nowak, M. G. |author6=Abdullah, A. |author7=Slater, H. D. |author8=Korstjens, A. H|author8-link=Amanda Korstjens|title=Sleeping trees and sleep-related behaviors of the siamang (''Symphalangus syndactylus'') in a tropical lowland rainforest, Sumatra, Indonesia |journal=Primates |volume=62 |issue=1 |pages=63–75 |doi=10.1007/s10329-020-00849-8 |doi-access=free |pmc=7813730}}</ref>

=== Voice ===
The calls of the grey-headed fish eagle include a gurgling ''awh-awhr'' and ''chee-warr'' repeated 5–6 times, an owlish ''ooo-wok'', ''ooo-wok'', ''ooo-wok'', a nasally honking ''uh-wuk'' and a loud high pitched scream.<ref name=Rasmussen /><ref name=Ferguson /> These begin as subdued low short notes each succeeding one more strongly upturned and more strident then previous then dying away again and are uttered from a perch or on the wing.<ref name=Rasmussen /><ref name=Ferguson /> Fledglings give a longer nasal ''uuuw-whaar'' that starts low and subdued then becomes, louder and higher and strident.<ref name=Rasmussen />  During the breeding season becomes quite vocal, with calls being loud and far carrying, often calling also at night.<ref name=Ferguson />

== Threats ==
Although not currently considered to be threatened with extinction, the population of grey-headed fish eagles is declining, the result of numerous and varied threats. The loss of suitable wetland habitat, deforestation, over-fishing, siltation, persecution, human disturbance and pollution resulting in a loss of nesting sites and reduced food supply.<ref name=iucn/><ref name=Ferguson /> Tingay et al.<ref name=Tingay2006 /><ref name=Tingay2010 /> noted that these statements are based mostly on anecdotal evidence, although their studies found a definitive negative link between human habitation and grey-headed fish eagle nest occupancy rates in Cambodia. Another critical threat to the Cambodian populations of the grey-headed fish eagle on the [[Tonlé Sap]] is the damming of the [[Mekong River]] for hydropower, which will possibly have adverse effects on critical flood regimes of the Tonlé Sap.<ref name=Tingay2006 /><ref name=Tingay2010 />

== Conservation status ==
The grey-headed fish eagle is currently listed as Near-Threatened on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name=iucn/> The population is estimated to be between 10,000–100,000 mature individuals on the basis that it may not exceed a five figure total.<ref name=iucn/> This estimate was completed in 2001 with poor data quality, combined with a marked decrease in populations is would be reasonable to assume that the number is closer to 10,000 and bordering on being classified Vulnerable.<ref name=iucn/> The population is spread out over 5 million km² and is now thought be only common locally, with moderate rapid population decline throughout its range.<ref name=iucn/>

Although there are no active conservation measures currently in place for the grey-headed fish eagle, there is an annual monitoring programme for the breeding population in the Prek Toal protected area at the Tonlé Sap Lake in Cambodia, which has been conducted each year since 2006.<ref name=Tingay2006 /> The programme provides baseline information on the ecology of the species and the status and distribution of the breeding population.<ref name=Tingay2010 /> A number of other conservation actions have also been proposed by the IUCN, these include surveys to reveal important areas and regularly monitor at various sites throughout its range, protect forest in areas known to be important to the species and conduct awareness campaigns involving local residents to engender pride in the species and encourage better care of wetland areas.<ref name=iucn/>
